---
title: "Statements and Quantifiers"
---

## Role

This page teaches the grammar of mathematical claims.

## Why It Matters

Many people think they do not understand proofs, when the real problem is that they are reading quantified statements too loosely.

## Intuition

A mathematical statement is not just a sentence. It also has scope:

- who or what the claim talks about
- whether the claim is universal or existential
- what assumptions are active

## Formal Core

The two quantifiers you must read precisely are:

- `\forall x \in S, P(x)` meaning “for every `x` in `S`, property `P(x)` holds”
- `\exists x \in S such that P(x)` meaning “there exists at least one `x` in `S` with property `P(x)`”

Negation rules matter:

- `\neg(\forall x, P(x))` becomes `\exists x` such that `\neg P(x)`
- `\neg(\exists x, P(x))` becomes `\forall x, \neg P(x)`

## Worked Example

Claim:

> For every real number `x`, if `x > 1` then `x^2 > 1`.

This is a universal statement with a conditional structure. To test your reading, ask:

- what is the domain?
- what is assumed?
- what must be proved?

## Application Lens

In research papers, assumptions are often compressed into a line like:

> Let `f` be `L`-smooth and `\mu`-strongly convex.

That line is already doing heavy logical work. If you read it casually, the rest of the theorem becomes blurry.

## Common Mistakes

- treating “for all” like “for many”
- forgetting the domain of the variable
- negating universal claims incorrectly

## Exercises

1. Negate the statement: “For every matrix `A`, there exists a vector `x` such that `Ax = 0`.”
2. Rewrite “No continuous function on `[0,1]` is unbounded” using quantifiers.
